meta.description: Tongan sentence grammar graph — Chapters 1-15 + 2C.1 (§§16-20: transitive, conjunctions, conditionals, cleft, noun classes) + 2C.2 (§§21-25: equational, aspect markers, reason clauses, benefactive) + 2C.3a (§26: cardinal numerals + toko personal counter) + 2C.3b (§27: fie + lava ʻo auxiliary verbs) + 2C.3c (§28: obligation totonu ke / kuo pau ke) + 2C.3d (§29: plural_marker reference node + irregular plural_form tagging pass) + 2C.3e (§30: post-verb directional particle slot) + 2C.4a (§31: comparative ange + superlative taha post-adjective slot) + 2C.4b (§32: existential entry point ʻoku ʻi ai ha + negation ʻoku ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha) + 2C.4c (§33: attributive_adjective postposed slot on prep_phrase + existential_noun) + 2C.4d (§34: permission tuku ke + optative ʻofa ke / ʻofa pē ke entry points reusing obligation_pronoun paradigm) + 2C.4e (§35: full 4×4 demonstrative grid reference node + emphatic discourse naʻa mo / kaeʻumaʻa reference node + ia added to demonstrative pronoun row) + 2C.4e-2 (§35: relative clause with ai — extension on demonstrative opens sub-walk tense→pronoun→verb, render pass auto-appends bare ai) + 2C.5b (§36: have construction ʻoku ʻi ai + possessive pronoun with polarity-driven indefinite/definite form selection) + 2C.7d (§50: exclamatory_ko_ka entry point — ko e X ka ko ha X! magnitude construction with FINISH_EXCLAMATION terminator) + 2C.7e (§50: exclamatory_me_a entry point — meʻa + modifier + ko e X evaluative admiration construction with FINISH_STATEMENT terminator)

entry_points.label: Make a statement
entry_points.description: Build a statement or question with a tense marker, pronoun, and verb

entry_points.label: Say where you are
entry_points.description: Say where someone is, with no action verb

entry_points.label: Express understanding or forgetting
entry_points.description: Use experiencer verbs like mahino (understand) and ngalo (forget)

entry_points.label: Make a suggestion
entry_points.description: Suggest doing something together using tau or ta

entry_points.label: Give a command (one person)
entry_points.description: Tell one person to do something — bare verb, no tense marker

entry_points.label: Give a command (group)
entry_points.description: Tell a group to do something using mou + verb

entry_points.label: Say don't
entry_points.description: Tell someone not to do something using 'oua te

entry_points.label: Say something isn't true
entry_points.description: Negate a statement: 'ikai te + pronoun, or 'ikai ke + verb + name

entry_points.label: Negate weather or conditions
entry_points.description: Negate impersonal statements (rain, wind, etc.) using 'ikai ke

entry_points.label: Identify something
entry_points.description: Say what something is: this is a book, that is a knife

entry_points.label: Say something isn't
entry_points.description: Negate identification: this is not a knife

entry_points.label: Ask what is that?
entry_points.description: Ask what something is using ko e hā

entry_points.label: Ask who?
entry_points.description: Ask who is doing something using ko hai

entry_points.label: Ask where is someone?
entry_points.description: Ask where someone is using ko fē

entry_points.label: Say what someone did (by name)
entry_points.description: Use a proper name as the subject — word order changes

entry_points.label: (purpose clause)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by `subordinator_ke_purpose`. Never surfaced in the Open Build entry-point chooser (its 'Subordinate' category is deliberately absent from CATEGORY_ORDER).

entry_points.label: (possessive phrase)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `possessive_pronoun` extension. Its sub-walk picks a possessive pronoun then a head noun; render time substitutes the paradigm form (ʻeku vs hoku) from the noun's possessive_class.

entry_points.label: (preposition + possessed thing)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `preposition_possessive` extension. Its sub-walk picks a preposition, then a possessive pronoun, then a head noun (e.g. `ki hoku fale`, `mei he ʻeku tohi`). Render time applies spec §36's `he` reappearance rule: ho-class keeps the base preposition (drop article), e-class appends `he` after the preposition. Never surfaced in Open Build category chooser.

entry_points.label: (possessor name)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `possessor_preposition` extension on prep_phrase.next (spec §22 `ʻa` / `ʻo` + name). Sub-walk picks a preposition (single word entry storing both paradigm forms under `possessive_forms: {e_class: ʻa, ho_class: ʻo}`) then a proper name. The head noun lives in the parent frame (the last prep_phrase step before the sub-walk begins); the render pass walks backwards from the preposition step to find the nearest preceding step whose word carries `possessive_class`, and substitutes the matching form. Never surfaced in Open Build category chooser.

entry_points.label: Say who did what to whom
entry_points.description: Transitive word order: verb first with ʻe-marked agent and ʻa-marked definite object (spec §16, Ch 19). Use this when both the agent and the acted-upon object are named.

entry_points.label: Put the spotlight on someone (It was X who...)
entry_points.description: Cleft construction: front the subject with Ko for emphasis, then a tense marker + relative pronoun + verb (spec §19, Ch 36).

entry_points.label: (pea second clause)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `clause_connector_pea` extension on verb.next. Parallel to the 2A.5 `clause_connector_ka` / `negation` pattern but routes to a full-statement sub-walk. allowed_terminators: [] forces explicit finishFrame to exit.

entry_points.label: (kapau conditional clause)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `subordinator_kapau` extension on verb.next. The sub-walk is a full statement clause (kapau → tense_marker → pronoun → verb …). allowed_terminators: [] forces finishFrame.

entry_points.label: (lolotonga temporal clause)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `subordinator_lolotonga` extension on verb.next. Shape is identical to the conditional_clause; different node for lexical disambiguation only.

entry_points.label: (serial ʻo clause)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `clause_connector_o` serial linker on command_verb.next (P1-A3, §17/§22 Ch 24 `Haʻu ʻo kai`, `ʻAlu ʻo ako`). Sub-walk is a single bare verb (clause_connector_o → verb). allowed_terminators: [] forces an explicit finishFrame to exit back to the parent command/clause, whose own FINISH terminator closes the sentence.

entry_points.label: (kae contrast clause)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `clause_connector_kae` connector on noun_subject_name.next (P1-A3, §17 Ch 24 `Naʻe ʻalu ʻa Sione kae nofo ʻa Mele`). Sub-walk is a tense-dropped noun-subject clause (clause_connector_kae → verb_ns → focus_marker → noun_subject_name). allowed_terminators: [] forces finishFrame back to the first clause.

entry_points.label: Say what someone is
entry_points.description: Equational sentence: Ko e + predicate noun (+ adjective) + postposed pronoun or ʻa + name (spec §21, Ch 16). Covers 'Ko e faiako au' / 'Ko e taʻahine fiefia ʻa Mele'. Predicate-first order only — subject-first emphasis (Ko Sione ko e faiako) is deferred.

entry_points.label: (he reason clause)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `clause_connector_he` extension on verb.next and object_phrase.next. Shape parallels pea_second_clause — the sub-walk is a full statement clause starting with tense_marker (spec §25, Ch 26 `he` = for, because).

entry_points.label: (koeʻuhi ke purpose clause)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `subordinator_koeuhi_ke` extension on verb.next. More emphatic form of `ke` purpose; shape parallels purpose_bare_verb with a bare purpose verb following the subordinator (spec §25, Ch 26 `koeʻuhi ke` = in order that, so that).

entry_points.label: (benefactive + name)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `benefactive_preposition_ma` extension. Sub-walk: benefactive preposition (single entry storing both paradigm forms as `possessive_forms: {e_class: maʻa, ho_class: moʻo}`) → proper name. Render pass walks backwards to find the nearest preceding step whose word carries `possessive_class` (the thing being given) and substitutes the matching maʻa / moʻo form. Same pattern as possessive_phrase_name for §22 `ʻa`/`ʻo`+name (spec §25, Ch 26).

entry_points.label: (benefactive pronoun)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the `benefactive_pronoun_fused` extension. Sub-walk picks one fused form (maʻaku/moʻoku, maʻau/moʻou, …). Each entry stores both paradigms in `possessive_forms`; walker selects at render time from the head noun's `possessive_class` (spec §25, Ch 26).

entry_points.label: Say someone should do something
entry_points.description: Obligation pattern with totonu ke = should / ought to (spec §28, Ch 23). Fixed-phrase totonu_phrase (ʻOku totonu ke / Naʻe totonu ke) → obligation_pronoun (ke-subordinator paradigm: u, ke, ne, nau, mau, tau, ta) → main verb. Spec §28 Entry point 1. Deferred: negation with ʻikai (ʻOku ʻikai totonu ke), emphatic negative with ʻoua naʻa, noun-subject pattern (flips to verb-first order), probability sense (translator flavor).

entry_points.label: Say someone must do something
entry_points.description: Obligation pattern with kuo pau ke = must / it is certain that (spec §28, Ch 23). Fixed-phrase pau_phrase (Kuo pau ke — the tense marker is structurally fixed at kuo per spec §28 Entry point 2) → obligation_pronoun (same ke-subordinator paradigm as obligation_should) → main verb. Deferred: noun-subject pattern and impersonal weather subject (Kuo pau ke vela he ʻaho ni).

entry_points.label: Say there is / there isn't
entry_points.description: Existential construction per spec §32 (Ch 31). Fixed-phrase existential_head (`ʻOku ʻi ai ha` / `Naʻe ʻi ai ha` / `Kuo ʻi ai ha` / `ʻE ʻi ai ha` for the affirmative, plus four `ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha` negated variants — 8 compound entries total) → existential_noun (common indefinite noun) → optional preposition for the ʻi + location continuation. The negation follows spec §32's `ʻikai ke` rather than `ʻikai te` pattern (the next word is the verb-like `ʻi ai`, not a preposed pronoun — same rule as §14 noun-subject negation). The indefinite article `ha` is structurally fixed per spec §32 and baked into each head entry rather than exposed as a separate node. Deferred: (a) the `ʻIo, ʻoku ʻi ai` short-form affirmative answer (drops the noun — separate exit-after-head shape); (b) the `fo'i` classifier (`ha fo'i niu` = a single coconut) — preposed on the noun slot, needs a `takes_fo_i_classifier` flag; (c) the `ka ʻi ai` = whenever there is conditional form (resolves §18 awareness item, belongs to §44 Ch 47); (d) relative clause stacking (`ʻOku ʻi ai ha tangata ʻokú ne ngāue heni`) — needs §35 relative_clause_extension; (e) `existential_compatible` filtering of which common nouns are natural existential subjects (current slice offers 6 hand-picked common nouns and defers the filter field).

entry_points.label: Say someone has / doesn't have something
entry_points.description: Have construction per spec §36 (Ch 29). Tongan has no verb 'to have'; instead the existential ʻi ai combines with possessive pronouns. 8 compound entries on have_head (4 affirmative tense variants + 4 negated variants) → reuses possessive_pronoun → possessive_head_noun chain from §22. Render pass reads two signals: (1) negated tag → indefinite forms; (2) FINISH_QUESTION on affirmative head → indefinite forms (questions about having use indefinite possessives). Affirmative + FINISH_STATEMENT → definite forms (default 2A.6 behavior). Deferred: (a) numeral extensions after the possessed noun (ʻeku hele ʻe ua); (b) location continuation (ʻi heni); (c) postposed possessives §37 (ʻaʻaku/ʻoʻoku paradigm, predicative use, whose-question ʻa hai/ʻo hai).

entry_points.label: Grant permission (let, allow)
entry_points.description: Permission construction per spec §34 (Ch 38). Fixed-phrase `Tuku ke` head → optional obligation_pronoun (the 2C.3c ke-subordinator paradigm — `u` / `ke` / `ne` / `nau` / `mau` / `tau` / `ta` / `mou`, with `mou` added in this slice to close the 2C.3c deferral) → main verb. Covers spec §34 examples `Tuku ké u fakamahino atu` (let me explain — pronoun path) and `Tuku ke ʻalu` (let him/her go — pronoun-less path where the subject is implicit or follows the verb as a name phrase). The tongan surface `Tuku ké u` with the acute accent on `ké` is a pick-time phrasal-accent display flourish that is NOT rendered in the data (same convention 2C.3c uses — the data stores the base form `Tuku ke u` and the accent pass belongs to the §46/Ch 44 phrasal-accent slice). The pronoun slot is OPTIONAL, unlike obligation_should / obligation_must where the pronoun is required — pronoun-less permission lets the subject surface as a name phrase after the verb per spec §34 `Tuku ke ʻalu ʻa e fefiné`. Deferred: (a) negative permission composition with §6 `ʻoua te` (`ʻOua te mou tuku ke lele noaʻia ʻa e fanga puaká`) — needs a composition mechanism between the `prohibition` entry point and `permission_tuku_ke`; (b) post-verb name-subject slot (`Tuku ke ʻalu ʻa e fefiné` = let the woman go) — flips to verb-first order with the §14/§16 noun-subject marker, same deferral as obligation_should / obligation_must; (c) the `let's` sense of English `let` — that maps to the existing §6 `suggestion` entry point with `tau`, not here.

entry_points.label: Express a hope (may, I hope that)
entry_points.description: Optative / hope construction per spec §34 (Ch 38). Fixed-phrase head ofa_ke_phrase (2 compound entries: `ʻOfa ke` = may / I hope that, and `ʻOfa pē ke` = the warmer / more earnest variant with the intensifier `pē`) → optional obligation_pronoun (same ke-subordinator paradigm as permission_tuku_ke) → main verb. Covers spec §34 examples `ʻOfa pē ke mou fiefia` (may you all be happy — pronoun path, this is why 2C.4d adds `mou` to obligation_pronoun) and `ʻOfa ke moʻui lelei ʻa e fonuá ni` (may this country prosper — pronoun-less path where the subject is a name phrase after the verb). Same optional-pronoun shape as permission_tuku_ke. Deferred: (a) the `ʻofa pē + tense marker` sub-pattern (`ʻOfa pē ʻoku ʻikai puke` = I hope he is not sick) where `pē` is followed by `ʻoku` / `kuo` / `te` / `ʻe` instead of `ke`, to express hope about an already-true or already-happened state — this is a different entry-point shape (the `ofa_pe_tm_phrase` head points at `tense_marker` rather than at the `ke` paradigm) and needs its own slice; (b) the formal hortative variant `tauange ke` (spec §34 awareness item, `register: formal`) — the shape is identical but awaits a `register` schema field to distinguish formal from neutral forms; (c) post-verb name-subject slot (same deferral as permission_tuku_ke).

entry_points.label: (relative clause)
entry_points.description: Internal entry point — the child frame pushed by the relative clause extension on demonstrative.next (spec §35, Ch 39). Sub-walk is a full verb-bearing clause (tense → pronoun → verb + extensions); the render pass auto-appends bare ʻaiʻ as the back-reference connector. Deferred: (a) ki ai / mei ai form-selection based on preposition tracking (2C.4e-3); (b) relative clause accent rendering (ʻokú ne, naʻá ku — phrasal accent on tense markers in relative context); (c) transitive-object relative clause (§19 pattern: definitive accent on verb, no ai — Ko e fefine eni naʻá ku ʻiló); (d) restriction to prevent nesting subordinate clauses inside the relative clause.

entry_points.label: Say this is mine (predicative possessive)
entry_points.description: Predicative possessive construction per spec §37 (Ch 37). The postposed possessive functions as the sentence's predicate after a tense marker, parallel to how adjectives function as verbs in Tongan (stative verb pattern). Structure: ʻOku + postposed_possessive + subject (demonstrative or ʻa e + noun). The tense marker is structurally fixed at ʻOku (present) per the spec §37 predicative table — other tenses are not attested. The postposed_possessive node from 2C.5c stores the full paradigm; the walker routes through it so the user picks a person/number/class form, then picks a subject. Deferred: (a) other tense markers for predicative possessive (naʻe, kuo, ʻe — not attested in spec §37); (b) negated predicative (ʻoku ʻikai ʻaʻaku eni — spec does not cover this but it's a natural extension); (c) pronominal adjective construction (preposed + postposed for emphasis, 2C.5e); (d) short-form substitution in predicative context.

entry_points.label: Exclaim about something (What a tremendous X!)
entry_points.description: Exclamatory magnitude construction per spec §50 (Ch 52). Pattern: ko e X ka ko ha X! — 'What a tremendous X!' The first clause uses the semi-definite e, the second uses the indefinite ha, creating emphasis through article contrast. Compound entries pre-bake the full doubled pattern because the graph walker has no template mechanism to render a single noun pick into the doubled construction. Deferred: (a) noun-pick + template rendering (requires walker render pass to auto-double the noun with e/ha article swap); (b) walker-level exclamatory register rendering.

entry_points.label: Admire something (X was a [quality] thing!)
entry_points.description: Evaluative admiration construction per spec §50 (Ch 52). Pattern: meʻa + modifier + ko e + noun. — 'X was a [modifier] thing!' Uses meʻa (thing) followed by a quality modifier (fakaʻofoʻofa = beautiful, mālie = wonderful, lelei = good) and a ko e + noun phrase to express admiration or wonder. Compound entries pre-bake the full pattern because the graph walker has no template mechanism to render modifier+noun picks into the construction. Unlike exclamatory_ko_ka which uses FINISH_EXCLAMATION (!), this pattern uses FINISH_STATEMENT (.) because the spec §50 examples consistently show period termination — the construction is semantically exclamatory but syntactically declarative. Deferred: (a) requires_quality_modifier field for modifier validation; (b) multi-node approach (modifier node → noun node); (c) walker-level template rendering; (d) additional modifier+noun combinations beyond the 6 spec examples.

prep_pronoun.label: Prepositional Pronoun
prep_pronoun.description: Choose the experiencer (to me, to you, etc.). This is the COMPLETION node of the experiencer clause (`ʻOku mahino kiate au` = it is understandable to me): the experiencer pronoun is the required final slot, so the clause-connector edges and the time-word extension hang here, not on verb_experiencer (graph-walker registers prep_pronoun in CLAUSE_COMPLETION_NODE_IDS so getClauseConnectorEdges surfaces them, exactly as noun_subject_name/command_verb/prohibition_verb do — P1-A3). P1-A1: a full tensed stative clause extends with a time word (`ʻOku mahino kiate au he ʻaho ni`, parallel to the spec stative `ʻOku lāʻā he ʻaho ni`, Ch 15:272); the time_word node already gates `ʻOku`→`ʻaho ni` / `Naʻe`→past words via its own depends_on:tense_marker_exp + valid_combinations, and the `he` before `ʻaho ni` is added by getRenderedPath per book Ch 4:89. Clause connectors (spec §22 line 1424: any first clause that could FINISH may take a connector; A1 cites `ka ʻoku ʻikai mahino kia Sione`) are wired for coordination/reason/condition/time. The two PURPOSE subordinators (ke_purpose, koeʻuhi ke) are deliberately omitted: purpose attaches to an agentive main verb, not to a stative experiencer (`*ʻOku mahino kiate au ke u ʻalu` is ill-formed).

postposed_pronoun.label: Emphasis
postposed_pronoun.description: Emphasize who is doing the action

neg_connector_ns.label: Connector
neg_connector_ns.description: ke connects ʻikai to the verb (noun subject negation)

subordinator_ke_purpose.label: Purpose
subordinator_ke_purpose.description: ke = in order to — opens a purpose sub-clause

clause_connector_ka.label: Contrast
clause_connector_ka.description: ka = but — joins a contrasting second clause (used before a tense marker)

possessive_pronoun.label: Possessive pronoun
possessive_pronoun.description: Whose thing? The same word renders differently based on the head noun's class — ʻeku/hoku (my), hoʻo/ho (your), ʻene/hono (his/her), ... The walker auto-selects the ʻe-class or ho-class form from the noun you pick next. The choice follows the doer/receiver principle (§36): ʻe-class marks the doer (ʻeku tokoni = my helping), ho-class marks the receiver (hoku tokoni = my being helped). Each singular/plural entry also carries indefinite_forms (§36 Ch 29) for the 'have' construction (ʻoku ʻi ai haʻaku X = do I have X); dual indefinite forms are not attested in §36 and are deferred.

possessive_head_noun.label: Possessed noun
possessive_head_noun.description: The thing someone owns, uses, or wears — each noun is tagged with the class the walker needs to render the right possessive form. Class follows the doer/receiver principle (§36 Ch 29): e_class = the possessor acts on, controls, or consumes the thing; ho_class = the thing shelters, characterizes, or acts on the possessor. Some nouns shift class by context (e.g. ʻeku vala = clothes I make vs hoku vala = clothes I wear) — these carry possessive_class on the word entry directly. Deferred: plant_type field (crops → e_class, trees → ho_class), family_term_class_override for parent/child exceptions.

pronominal_adjective.label: Pronominal adjective (emphasis)
pronominal_adjective.description: Emphatic postposed possessive short form per spec §37 (Ch 37). The pronominal adjective construction doubles the possessive: a preposed possessive already identifies ownership; this postposed short form stresses EXACTLY who the owner is. Pattern: [preposed_possessive] + [noun + definitive_accent] + [short_form]. The node has a single placeholder entry; the render pass auto-selects the matching short form by reading the preceding possessive_pronoun's person/number and the possessive_head_noun's possessive_class from the postposed_possessive paradigm data. The definitive accent is also auto-applied to the head noun. Spec §37 examples: Hono soté ʻona (HIS shirt), ʻEku paʻangá ʻaku (MY money), Hoʻo tohí ʻau (YOUR book). Deferred: (a) dual/plural emphasis forms only produce the long postposed form since no short_form exists on those entries — the render pass falls back to tongan; (b) person/number agreement validation (currently the user picks the placeholder, render pass auto-fills; a future slice could validate that the user hasn't somehow walked into an inconsistent state); (c) ʻoku short-form / ʻoku tense-marker disambiguation entries; (d) mixed-class handling.

possessor_preposition.label: Possessor preposition
possessor_preposition.description: ʻa (e-class) or ʻo (ho-class) — the single entry below stores both paradigm forms under possessive_forms; the walker auto-selects from the head noun's possessive_class at render time. Parallel to possessive_pronoun: user never picks the class directly.

possessor_name.label: Possessor name
possessor_name.description: The proper name of the possessor — Sione, Mele, etc. Rendered as-is; class comes from the head noun, not the name.

tense_marker_tr.label: Tense Marker (transitive)
tense_marker_tr.description: Choose when the action happens. Note: Naʻe / ʻE (not Naʻa / Te) because the next word is a verb, not a preposed pronoun — same rule as tense_marker_ns in §14.

verb_tr.label: Verb (transitive)
verb_tr.description: Transitive verbs that take a definite ʻa-marked object (spec §16).

ma_potential_form.english: able to manage, able to do

object_phrase.label: Definite object
object_phrase.description: Choose what was acted upon. Entries are stored bare — the absolutive marker ʻa, the definite article e (for common nouns), and the final-vowel accent (definitive_accent_form) are all applied at render time via getRenderedPath. Personal names surface as `ʻa Sione`; common nouns as `ʻa e iká` etc.

agent_phrase.label: Agent
agent_phrase.description: Choose who did the action. Entries are stored bare — the ergative marker ʻe, the definite article `he` (for common nouns, used after ʻe), and the final-vowel accent are all applied at render time. Personal names surface as `ʻe Sione`; common nouns as `ʻe he tamasiʻí` etc.

clause_connector_pea.label: And then
clause_connector_pea.description: pea = and then — joins a sequential second clause (spec §17, Ch 24). Pushes a `pea_second_clause` child frame. Two paths: (1) full form with tense_marker, (2) tense-drop shortcut (peá + pronoun) that infers tense from the main clause.

clause_connector_pea_serial.label: And then (command)
clause_connector_pea_serial.description: pea = and then — the IMPERATIVE-chain form that joins a second bare verb to a command (spec §17, Ch 24 `Mohe pea ngāue`, `Haʻu pea kai`: two commands, each able to stand alone). Distinct from clause_connector_pea (statement/noun-subject), which keeps the pronoun after pea (`Naʻá ku lotu peá u mohe`, Ch 24:23/138-160) and so never drops to a bare verb. The second verb is bare (no tense, no pronoun); the child frame uses the serial_clause entry point (allowed_terminators []).

clause_connector_o.label: And (serial ʻo)
clause_connector_o.description: ʻo = and / and then — the serial-verb linker that joins a second bare verb to a command or clause (spec §17/§22, Ch 24). Examples: Haʻu ʻo kai (come and eat), ʻAlu ʻo ako (go and study), Mohe pea ngāue uses pea but ʻo is the tighter serial form. The second verb is bare (no tense marker), parallel to the ke-purpose sub-walk; the child frame uses the serial_clause entry point (allowed_terminators []), so it exits via finishFrame back to the command/clause whose FINISH terminator closes the sentence.

clause_connector_kae.label: While / and (kae)
clause_connector_kae.description: kae = and / while / but — joins a contrasting second clause with a DIFFERENT subject directly to a bare verb (tense-drop), the noun-subject counterpart of ka (spec §17, Ch 24). Example: Naʻe ʻalu ʻa Sione kae nofo ʻa Mele (Sione went while Mele stayed). The second clause is a tense-dropped noun-subject clause (bare verb_ns + ʻa + name); the child frame uses the contrast_clause_kae entry point (allowed_terminators []), so it exits via finishFrame back to the first clause whose FINISH terminator closes the sentence.

tense_marker_weather.label: Tense Marker
tense_marker_weather.description: Choose when for an affirmative impersonal-weather clause (ʻoku ʻuha = it is raining). Reached only inside the `he` reason clause (spec §25, Ch 26 `he ʻoku ʻuha` = because it is raining); the affirmative impersonal weather construction has no top-level entry point, so this node exists purely as a he-reason branch. Words are stored lowercase because the clause is always non-initial.

subordinator_kapau.label: If
subordinator_kapau.description: kapau = if — opens a conditional clause (spec §18, Ch 30). Requires a tense marker in the dependent clause, so the walker auto-advances to tense_marker as a required next.

subordinator_lolotonga.label: While
subordinator_lolotonga.description: lolotonga = while / during — opens a temporal clause (spec §18, Ch 30). Same shape as subordinator_kapau — a full-statement dependent clause.

ko_emphatic.label: Ko (emphatic)
ko_emphatic.description: Ko — fronts the subject of a cleft sentence for emphasis (spec §19, Ch 36).

subject_phrase.label: Fronted subject
subject_phrase.description: Choose who is emphasized — a proper name, pronoun, or common-noun phrase.

the man (with pity, affection, or humility)
the woman (with pity, affection, or humility)

tense_marker_cleft.label: Tense Marker (cleft)
tense_marker_cleft.description: After a fronted subject, use the naʻe/ʻoku/kuo/ʻe forms — not naʻa/te — because the next word is a relative pronoun, not a preposed pronoun.

pronoun_relative.label: Relative pronoun
pronoun_relative.description: Refers back to the fronted subject — the walker filters the list down to the single form that agrees with the subject (3sg names → ne, au → ku, koe → ke, plurals → nau).

verb_cleft.label: Verb (cleft)
verb_cleft.description: Choose the action or state. Transitive verbs expose a definite-object extension; intransitive verbs expose location and time extensions directly.

ko_e_equational.label: Ko e (equational)
ko_e_equational.description: Ko e = (it is) a / the — opens an equational sentence (spec §21, Ch 16). Same surface form as ko_e_fixed but the continuation is an equational subject (postposed pronoun or ʻa + name), not a demonstrative.

next.label: Add diminutive (kiʻi = small)
next.context_hint: Mark the noun as small before adding it

ki_i_diminutive.label: Diminutive (preposed)
ki_i_diminutive.description: kiʻi = small — placed before the noun (spec §21, Ch 16). Postposed siʻisiʻi attaches after the noun via the equational_noun.next extension.

equational_noun.label: Predicate noun
equational_noun.description: The noun that identifies or classifies the subject — teacher, worker, doctor, etc. Each entry carries noun_class + possessive_class so the render pass and downstream benefactive sub-walks can resolve classes correctly.

next.label: Add adjective (happy, strong, old...)
next.context_hint: Describe the predicate noun

next.label: Add diminutive (siʻisiʻi = small)
next.context_hint: Mark it as small with postposed siʻisiʻi

modifier_eq.label: Adjective (equational)
modifier_eq.description: A postposed adjective that describes the predicate noun (spec §21, Ch 16). Separate word list from the verb-modifier node because the adjectives and edge shape differ.

si_isi_i_postposed.label: Diminutive (postposed)
si_isi_i_postposed.description: siʻisiʻi = small — placed after the noun or adjective (spec §21, Ch 16). Can stack with preposed kiʻi on the same noun for an intensifying 'very small' effect ('Ko e kiʻi vaka siʻisiʻi.').

equational_subject.label: Equational subject
equational_subject.description: The subject of an equational sentence — either a postposed pronoun (au, koe, ia) or ʻa + proper name. With pronoun subjects the focus marker ʻa drops away (Ch 16 rule); with proper-name subjects ʻa is retained. Each entry bakes the correct surface form so no render-time substitution is needed.

aspect_marker.label: Aspect marker
aspect_marker.description: Preposed marker that tells WHAT STAGE the action is in — still (kei), already (ʻosi), not yet (teʻeki / teʻeki ai), currently (lolotonga), again (toe), just / not until (toki) — plus the §24 frequency/distribution markers that share the preposed slot: toutou (repeatedly) and taki taha (each one). Placed between the pronoun and the verb in the statement chain (spec §24, Ch 22). Postposed markers split into sibling nodes: aspect_marker_post (leva) and aspect_marker_post_frequency (ai pē). tuʻo + numeral lives in tuo_numeral. The taki + numeral distributive (taki ua = two each) attaches before a NOUN, not a verb, so it is deferred until a noun-modifier slot exists.

aspect_marker_post.label: Aspect marker (postposed)
aspect_marker_post.description: leva = immediately / at once — placed AFTER the verb (spec §24, Ch 22). Separate node from aspect_marker because the position rule differs; attached to verb.next as an optional extension.

next.label: Add location/direction
next.context_hint: Say where

aspect_marker_post_frequency.label: Frequency marker (postposed)
aspect_marker_post_frequency.description: ai pē = as always, as usual, still — postposed clause-modifier signalling unchanged continuation (spec §24, Ch 22, 2C.2b follow-up). Position rule differs from aspect_marker_post leva: ai pē tends to sit clause-final after the verb (intransitive: ʻOku fakalaka ai pē ʻa Tonga ki muʻa) or after the object (transitive-passive-ish: ʻOku kei fai ai pē e ngāué). Attached to both verb.next and object.next so it can hang off either parent. Sibling of aspect_marker_post — same postposed slot, different semantics.

next.label: Add location/direction
next.context_hint: Say where

tuo_numeral.label: Count of times (tuʻo + numeral)
tuo_numeral.description: tuʻo + numeral = N times — postposed quantifier counting how many times the action happens (spec §24, Ch 22, 2C.2b follow-up). Stored as compound entries (tuʻo taha, tuʻo ua, tuʻo tolu, tuʻo fā) plus the open-ended quantifiers tuʻo lahi (many times), tuʻo siʻi (a few times), and the question form tuʻo fiha (how many times?). Attached to verb.next as a postposed extension. The ordinal use (Ko e tuʻo tolu eni = this is the third time) requires equational integration with ko_equational and is deferred — same rationale as the §21 subject-first equational deferral noted in 2C.2.

next.label: Add location/direction
next.context_hint: Say where

clause_connector_he.label: Because
clause_connector_he.description: he = for, because — joins a reason clause to the preceding statement (spec §25, Ch 26). The reason sub-walk is a full statement clause; the user picks any of three clause shapes via parallel tense_marker targets (pronoun-subject, noun-subject, negation). multi-walker's groupBranchingExtensions merges the three under the shared 'Tense Marker' label so the picker shows a single category with Naʻa/ʻOku/Kuo/Te (pronoun-subject) plus Naʻe/ʻE (noun-subject) plus ʻikai-shape variants. pickWord's first-extension-wins fallback routes each word to the branch whose target node accepts it: Naʻa → tense_marker (pronoun slot), Naʻe → tense_marker_ns (verb slot), etc.

subordinator_koeuhi_ke.label: So that
subordinator_koeuhi_ke.description: koeʻuhi ke = so that, in order that — emphatic form of ke purpose (spec §25, Ch 26). Same shape as subordinator_ke_purpose: opens a bare-purpose-verb sub-walk. In everyday speech the koeʻuhi can drop before ke, leaving just ke (that shorter form is already covered by subordinator_ke_purpose).

benefactive_preposition_ma.label: Benefactive preposition
benefactive_preposition_ma.description: maʻa (e-class) or moʻo (ho-class) — the single entry below stores both paradigm forms under possessive_forms; the render pass selects at render time from the nearest preceding step whose word carries possessive_class (the thing being given / intended). Parallel to possessor_preposition for §22 ʻa / ʻo + name — user never picks the class directly.

benefactive_pronoun_fused.label: Benefactive pronoun
benefactive_pronoun_fused.description: Fused benefactive + possessive pronoun — maʻaku/moʻoku, maʻau/moʻou, maʻana/moʻona, etc. Each entry stores both paradigm forms under possessive_forms; the walker selects at render time from the nearest preceding step whose word carries possessive_class. Pattern parallels possessive_pronoun §22 auto-selection, but the fusion happens on the preposition itself rather than a separate possessor-preposition slot.

object_phrase_cleft.label: Definite object (cleft)
object_phrase_cleft.description: Same absolutive-marked noun as the transitive object_phrase, attached to a cleft verb as an optional extension. Entries are stored bare and render-time applies `ʻa e <accent>` / `ʻa <name>` — the duplicated list (separate from object_phrase) keeps each node's slot-shape independent.

numeral.label: Numeric count
numeral.description: ʻe + cardinal numeral — the numeric particle ʻe attaches a count to a non-personal noun (spec §26, Ch 20). Each entry bakes the ʻe particle into the tongan field so rendering is literal; no walker / schema changes are needed for this data slice. Attached as a post-noun extension on object.next. Terminal slot in its OWN next, but NOT a dead end: numeral is always reached as an extension off the completable `object` node, so after the count the walker auto-pops back to object's full menu (modifiers incl. `pē`, time_word, preposition, companion, clause connectors). `Naʻa ku kai mā ʻe ono pē` (only six) and `Naʻa ku kai mā ʻe ono ʻaneafi` (six, yesterday) already build that way. P1-A2 deliberately leaves numeral.next FINISH-only for exactly this reason: adding native `pē` / `time_word` edges here would give the node its own non-terminator extensions, suppressing the auto-pop and shrinking the menu to just those two (the §A over-restriction). The §26 `pē` continuation that DOES need wiring is the personal-count predicate (`ʻOku nau toko ono pē`) — see `count_enclitic_pe` and `personal_count`. Deferred: the `compound_numeral` mā-joined form (hongofulu mā taha), the definitive-accent rendering on the final numeral (ʻa e kato ʻe onó), toko-vs-ʻe animacy auto-selection, and the ordinal `ko hono + numeral` pattern — all §26.

fie_aux.label: Auxiliary: want to (fie)
fie_aux.description: fie = want to, wish to — a pre-verb auxiliary that slots between the pronoun and the main verb (spec §27, Ch 21). Structurally mirrors `preposed_modifier` (faʻa): single word, `required_next` edge to verb. The sentence `ʻOku ou fie ako` = 'I want to study' walks tense_marker → pronoun → fie_aux → verb. Spec §27 Node 1. Deferred: (a) the fie + uncomplimentary-adjective sub-pattern (fie poto, fie lahi, fie lelei = conceited, show-off, goody-goody) — requires the `fie_uncomplimentary` flag on adjective entries and a translator hook for the insulting gloss; (b) aspect+auxiliary co-occurrence (ʻOku ʻikai te u kei fie ʻalu) — spec §27 says the order is `aspect + auxiliary + verb`, but current pronoun.next exposes aspect_marker and fie_aux as exclusive siblings, so the user can only pick one per sentence. Wiring aspect → fie_aux → verb (or exposing fie_aux on aspect_marker.next) is a later slice.

lava_o_aux.label: Auxiliary: be able to (lava ʻo)
lava_o_aux.description: lava ʻo = be able to, can — a pre-verb auxiliary that slots between the pronoun and the main verb (spec §27, Ch 21). Structurally distinct from fie_aux because it requires the conjunction ʻo between the auxiliary and the main verb; encoded here as a single compound word `lava ʻo` to match the 2C.3a / 2C.2b compound-form convention (no walker change needed). Required_next to verb. The sentence `ʻOku ou lava ʻo ʻalu` = 'I can go' walks tense_marker → pronoun → lava_o_aux → verb. Spec §27 Node 2. Deferred: (a) lava + noun-subject construction (`Naʻe lava ʻa Siale ʻo hiva lelei` / `ʻE lava ʻe Pita ʻo fakatau ʻa e meʻakai`) — this flips to verb-first order with the subject marker (ʻa or ʻe) determined by the second verb's transitivity, which is a separate entry-point shape (spec §27 Node 2 noun-subject case); (b) lava + negation (`ʻOku ʻikai te u lava ʻo ʻalu`) — the negation entry point would need lava_o_aux attached on its pronoun slot. (c) aspect+auxiliary co-occurrence — same issue as fie_aux.

personal_count.label: Personal count
personal_count.description: toko + cardinal numeral — the people-counter prefix toko replaces the numeric particle ʻe when the count is of people (spec §26, Ch 20). Each entry is the pre-compound toko + numeral, mirroring the sibling `numeral` node's compound-form approach. Attached as an alternative-path extension on pronoun.next (`ʻOku nau toko tolu` = they are three people), where it is taken in BRANCHING mode (the pronoun anchor has no FINISH edge) — so before P1-A2 the count predicate was a hard FINISH-only dead-end (`ʻOku nau toko tolu` offered only Finish). P1-A2 (plan A2, spec §26 `ʻOku toko ono pē` / `ʻOku toko hiva pē`) wires two optional continuations onto this completion node: the enclitic `count_enclitic_pe` (`pē` = only/just → `ʻOku nau toko ono pē`) and a trailing `time_word` (`ʻOku nau toko tolu he ʻaho ni`, the `he` added by getRenderedPath per Ch 4:89). Both are extending-mode pushes that auto-pop back here and are recorded in extensionsTaken, so neither pē nor the time word can repeat, and either pick order renders grammatically (`toko ono pē he ʻaho ni` / `toko ono he ʻaho ni pē`). The sibling `numeral` node (the non-personal `ʻe + cardinal` count) is intentionally NOT given these edges: it is reached only as an extension off the completable `object` node, where the auto-pop already restores object's full menu (its modifier words include `pē`, plus time_word / preposition / connectors), so wiring native pē/time_word edges onto numeral.next would suppress that auto-pop and narrow the menu (the §A over-restriction this plan removes). Deferred: the tokolahi / tokosiʻi / tuʻutamaki quantifier words, the `toko fiha` interrogative, the `(noun) + ʻe + toko + numeral` transitive-object form (`Ko e tamaiki ʻe toko tolu`), and attachment on tense_marker.next for the subject-less `ʻOku toko ono pē` pattern (no pronoun — would require making the pronoun slot non-required, deferred) — all §26.

next.label: Add pē (only / just that many)
next.context_hint: Say there are ONLY that many — ʻOku nau toko ono pē (there are only six of them)

next.label: Add time word (when)
next.context_hint: Say when — he ʻaho ni (today) with ʻOku; ʻaneafi / ʻanepō (past) with Naʻa / Naʻe

count_enclitic_pe.label: Enclitic pē (only / just)
count_enclitic_pe.description: P1-A2 (plan A2, spec §26): the enclitic `pē` = 'only / just' that closes a count predicate — `ʻOku toko hiva pē` ('only nine'), `ʻOku toko ono pē` ('there are only six'). Reached as an optional extension off `personal_count` (the toko + numeral people-count predicate) so `ʻOku nau toko ono pē` builds; before P1-A2 personal_count was a FINISH-only branching-mode dead-end. TERMINAL node (next = FINISH only): keeping it terminal means pē resolves AT the personal_count anchor — picking pē pushes a one-step sub-frame that auto-pops back, where extensionsTaken records it (so `pē pē` is impossible) and the sibling `time_word` is still offered for `ʻOku nau toko ono pē he ʻaho ni`. A trailing time word is therefore reached via the personal_count anchor, not via this node's next, which also blocks a second time word. Deliberately NOT routed from `numeral`: a bare `ʻe + cardinal` count is reached only as an extension off the completable `object` node, where the auto-pop already restores object's full menu (its modifier word list contains `pē`, so `mā ʻe ono pē` builds, plus time_word / preposition / connectors), so adding native edges onto numeral.next would suppress that auto-pop and narrow the menu (the §A over-restriction this plan removes). Deferred: the subject-less `ʻOku toko ono pē` with no pronoun (needs personal_count on tense_marker.next); `pē` after the tokolahi / tokosiʻi quantifier words (`ʻOku nau tokosiʻi pē`); the `toko fiha` interrogative.

totonu_phrase.label: Obligation head (totonu)
totonu_phrase.description: Fixed-phrase head for the `obligation_should` entry point (spec §28 Entry point 1, Ch 23). Two compound forms stored as single tongan fields — `ʻOku totonu ke` (present, 'should / ought to') and `Naʻe totonu ke` (past, 'should have'). The tense marker plus `totonu ke` subordinator clause-opener are baked into each entry, same compound-form trick as 2C.3a / 2C.3b. Required_next to obligation_pronoun (the ke-subordinator pronoun paradigm), which then feeds into verb. Deferred: (a) negation with ʻikai inserted between tense marker and totonu (`ʻOku ʻikai totonu ke ke mohe`) — requires a sibling totonu_phrase_negated node or an insertion hook; (b) emphatic negative with `ʻoua naʻa` inside the ke-clause (`ʻOku totonu ke ʻoua naʻá ke ʻita`) — spec §28 sub-pattern; (c) noun-subject pattern (`ʻOku totonu ke ngāue lahi ʻa Mele`) which flips to verb-first order with the §14/§16 noun-subject marker; (d) probability sense rendering (translator flavor, no structural change).

pau_phrase.label: Obligation head (pau)
pau_phrase.description: Fixed-phrase head for the `obligation_must` entry point (spec §28 Entry point 2, Ch 23). Single compound entry `Kuo pau ke` (= 'must / it is certain that') — the tense marker is structurally fixed at `kuo` per spec §28 since the perfect aspect is part of the construction (literally 'it has become definite that'). Required_next to obligation_pronoun → verb. Deferred: noun-subject pattern (`Kuo pau ke haʻu ʻa Sione`) and impersonal weather subject (`Kuo pau ke vela he ʻaho ni`) — both flip to the same verb-first noun-subject order deferred for totonu_phrase.

directional.label: Directional particle
directional.description: Post-verb directional particle slot per spec §30 (Ch 28). Six words in two groups: three person-oriented (mai = toward speaker / 1st, atu = toward listener / 2nd, ange = toward 3rd), three physical (hake = up, hifo = down, holo = around with no fixed destination). Attached on verb.next. directional.next allows continuation to object / time_word / preposition so spec examples like `Naʻá ku ʻave mai ʻa e valá mei he falé` (`ʻave` + `mai` + object + prep_phrase) walk end-to-end. Docs.4 catalog enforcement: `default_taken` — the walker's extensionsTaken mechanism tracks 'taken once' cardinality; pick-time SLOT-ORDER enforcement ('directional must come before object / prep_phrase / time_word per spec §30 line 3713') is NOT active in this slice. The current walker treats post-verb extensions as unordered siblings, so a user could pick `object` before `directional` and produce a wrong-order sentence. Enforcing Rule 5 at pick time is a separate walker-behavior slice. Deferred: (a) `verb_excludes_directionals` array field — spec §30 documents that haʻu (come) excludes all directionals except mai (you use `ʻalu hake`/`ʻalu hifo` instead), but this would require a new optional field in the schema allowlist; (b) ange homonymy disambiguation — spec §30 notes ange also means 'more' in comparative contexts (Ch 27, §31) and the walker should decide by what precedes it; (c) emphatic command form with definitive accent on the directional (`ʻAlu hifó!`) — part of the phrasal accent system finalized in Ch 44 / §46; (d) directional + ʻo conjunction (`Naʻa nau ō hake ʻo ngāue`) — works composably once the §17 ʻo connector is extended.

next.label: Add object (what)
next.context_hint: Say what was moved — after a directional, the object still follows

next.label: Add time word (when)
next.context_hint: Say when the directional motion happened

next.label: Add location / destination
next.context_hint: Say the source or destination — mei he falé (from the house), ki he mātāfagá (to the beach)

plural_marker.label: Plural marker
plural_marker.description: Pre-noun optional slot holding the five plural / dual markers introduced in spec §29 (Ch 25): `ngaahi` (general plural, default for animacy:thing), `kau` (people plural, default for animacy:person — also the §26 'group of' marker per spec §29's cross-cutting note), `fanga` (animal plural, default for animacy:animal — also forced for any noun preceded by the §21 diminutive `kiʻi`), `ʻū` (alternate plural for things, especially small groups — offered alongside ngaahi), and `ongo` (dual marker for exactly two of any noun). This slice declares the node as reference data only — it is deliberately NOT wired as an extension edge in the current graph. Wire-up requires splitting article from noun in every existing noun-bearing word list (object, prep_phrase, equational_noun, possessive_head_noun, object_phrase_cleft, etc.) which currently store article + noun as a single compound tongan field (`e ika`, `ha talo`). That refactor is a separate slice. Once wired, the walker will insert plural_marker between article and noun and auto-select the marker from the noun's `animacy` tag (Rule 3 pattern). Deferred alongside: (a) the §29 `kotoa` postposed quantifier (`ʻa e fale kotoa` = all the houses) — a separate postposed-modifier extension node; (b) the `ambiguous_animacy` flag for words like `hiva`/`ako`/`lotu`/`ngāue` that can name either a person or an activity; (c) the `fafine` dual / plural courtesy split (2 = fafine, 3+ = kau fefiné); (d) walker auto-substitution of the `plural_form` field (that's a 2A.7 render-pass slice; this 2C.3d slice only TAGS the field, it doesn't activate substitution).

obligation_pronoun.label: Pronoun (after ke subordinator)
obligation_pronoun.description: The 8 ke-subordinator pronoun forms shared by `obligation_should`, `obligation_must`, `permission_tuku_ke`, `optative_ofa_ke`, and (spec §25) the wider ke-subordinator pattern. 1sg = u (same form as after kuo/te), 2sg = ke (the pronoun ke, yielding the doubled `ke ke` shape spec §28 documents: first ke is subordinator, second is pronoun), 3sg = ne, 3pl = nau, 1pl.excl = mau, 1pl.incl = tau, 2pl = mou (added in 2C.4d because spec §34 `ʻOfa pē ke mou fiefia` requires it — 2C.3c originally listed only the 7 spec §28 forms and deferred the extras), 1du.incl = ta. No constraints block: the depends_on / valid_combinations the main `pronoun` node uses exists to filter tense_marker pairings, but this node has a single upstream (the obligation_head / tuku_ke_phrase / ofa_ke_phrase each already commit to a specific tense or are tense-neutral) so no filtering is needed. Required_next to verb. Deferred: the dual-number forms `ma` (we two excl) and `mo` (you two) — spec §28 and spec §34 examples do not attest these two forms in the ke-subordinator slot so they await a future completeness slice.

comparative_ange.label: Comparative marker (ange)
comparative_ange.description: Post-adjective comparative marker per spec §31 (Ch 27). Single word `ange` = `more`. Attached as an extension on verb.next with a `verb_has_tag: adjective` gate so it is only offered after an adjective / stative verb; spec §31 line 4055-4056 documents that the SAME word `ange` is the directional particle after an action verb (§30 / 2C.3e) and the comparative after an adjective (this slice). The gate serves to HIDE comparative_ange after action verbs; the inverse gate on directional is NOT added in this slice because it would change existing 2C.3e behavior and the disambiguation-by-preceding-POS rule is documented as engine-deferred. Attached as a sibling of `directional` on verb.next, positioned between directional and modifier so picking order follows spec §31's adjective-first construction. `comparative_ange.next` includes FINISH × 2 plus an optional `preposition` continuation so the standard 'than X' phrase walks through the existing preposition → prep_phrase chain with noun-class auto-rendering per spec §31's `'i` / `'ia` / `'i he` rule (Rule 3). Deliberate deferrals (documented for future slices): (a) comparison modifiers `kiʻi` / `fuʻu` / `toe` that wrap around the adjective to form `kiʻi lahi ange` (a little bigger) — spec §31 calls these 'preposed modifiers' and they need a new pre-adjective slot; (b) `requires_lahi_in_comparative` boolean flag on adjective entries like `ʻita` (you cannot say `ʻoku ʻita ange`, only `ʻoku ʻita lahi ange`) — requires a new optional field in the schema allowlist; (c) `tatau mo` / `fai kehekehe mo` same/different constructions (different structural shape); (d) `meimei` / `mei` / `matuʻaki` preposed intensifiers (sibling of `preposed_modifier`, waiting for a parallel preposed slot); (e) `hangē` comparison clauses with `ʻo hangē ha` / `ʻo hangē ko` / `ʻo hangē + clause` (new structural shape, cross-cuts §17 conjunction); (f) `ʻangeʻange` / `laka ʻi` surpass verbs with translator word-order swap (new field `surpass_verb` and translator hook); (g) `huanoa` / `lahi pē` / `tautefito ki` discourse connectors; (h) `ange` homonymy disambiguation at pick time (both comparative_ange and directional are currently offered after an adjective since directional is ungated — user picks).

next.label: Add 'than' phrase (ʻi + compared)
next.context_hint: Say what you are comparing against — ʻi + the other thing (noun-class auto-rendering: ʻi / ʻia / ʻi he per Rule 3)

next.label: ʻa + subject (who did it)
next.context_hint: Attach noun subject after comparative

superlative_taha.label: Superlative marker (taha)
superlative_taha.description: Post-adjective superlative marker per spec §31 (Ch 27). Single word `taha` = `the most`. Attached as an extension on verb.next with the same `verb_has_tag: adjective` gate as `comparative_ange`, positioned immediately after comparative_ange. Spec §31 line 3843 documents that there is NO 'than' phrase with the superlative — the thing described is implicitly 'the most within its group' — so superlative_taha.next is terminal (FINISH only). Common forms spec §31 lists: `lelei taha` / `lahi taha` / `mālohi taha` / `vave taha` / `sai taha` — all of these walk by picking the adjective at the verb slot then attaching taha. Deliberate deferrals: (a) the `lahi taha` vs `ange` lexical overlap (`lahi taha` = the largest, `lahi ange` = larger) — both are reachable via the same verb+adjective mechanism so no disambiguation is needed; (b) the reduplicated `ngātaha` / intensifier variants.

next.label: ʻa + subject (who did it)
next.context_hint: Attach noun subject after superlative

existential_head.label: Existential head (ʻoku ʻi ai ha / ʻoku ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha)
existential_head.description: Fixed-phrase head for the `existential` entry point (spec §32 Ch 31). 8 compound entries stored as single tongan fields: 4 affirmative tense variants (`ʻOku ʻi ai ha` present, `Naʻe ʻi ai ha` past, `Kuo ʻi ai ha` perfect, `ʻE ʻi ai ha` future) and 4 negated variants (`ʻOku ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha`, `Naʻe ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha`, `Kuo ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha`, `ʻE ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha`). The past affirmative is `naʻe` rather than `naʻa` and the future affirmative is `ʻe` rather than `te` because the next word is the verb-like `ʻi ai`, not a preposed pronoun — same rule spec §14 documents for noun-subject sentences. The negated form uses `ʻikai ke` rather than `ʻikai te` for the same reason (spec §32 line 4149). The indefinite article `ha` is structurally fixed per spec §32 — existential sentences ask whether something exists, not which specific one — so `ha` is baked into each compound entry rather than exposed as a separate `ha_indefinite` node. Each entry is tagged with `existential_head` plus `affirmative` or `negated`. Required_next to existential_noun. Same compound-form trick 2C.3a uses for `ʻe + cardinal`, 2C.3b uses for `lava ʻo`, and 2C.3c uses for `ʻOku totonu ke`. Deliberate deferrals: (a) `ʻIo, ʻoku ʻi ai` short-form affirmative answer (drops the noun completely — needs a FINISH-after-head shape); (b) the `ʻi ai` back-reference use in non-existential contexts (spec §32 cross-cutting note) — that belongs to §35 relative_clause_extension and §19 cleft contexts where `ai` replaces a locative phrase; (c) the `ka ʻi ai` = whenever there is conditional form (spec §32 awareness item, resolves in §44 Ch 47); (d) the `ʻaki` instrumental back-reference parallel to `ai` (spec §32 awareness item, belongs to §38 Ch 33).

words.tongan: ʻOku ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha
words.english: there is no / there are no

words.tongan: Naʻe ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha
words.english: there was no / there were no

words.tongan: Kuo ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha
ʻE ʻikai ke ʻi ai ha

next.label: Pick the thing that exists (or doesn't)

existential_noun.label: Existential noun
existential_noun.description: Indefinite common noun slot for the `existential` entry point (spec §32 Ch 31). 6 common nouns spec §32 uses in its example sentences (`tangata` = man, `fefine` = woman, `taha` = one / anyone, `vaka` = boat, `kātoanga` = celebration, `meʻakai` = food). No article baked in — the `ha` indefinite is already part of the existential_head compound entry. Each word tagged with `noun_class: common` so future Rule 3 passes can render it correctly; `animacy` and `possessive_class` tagged alongside to match the 2B.2 / 2B.3 invariants asserted elsewhere. The `next` edges allow FINISH × 2 plus an optional `preposition` continuation so spec examples like `ʻOku ʻi ai ha tangata ʻi heni` (there is a man here) walk through the existing preposition → prep_phrase chain with the same noun-class auto-rendering Rule 3 enforces elsewhere. Deliberate deferrals: (a) `existential_compatible` filter field on the main `prep_phrase.words` / `object.words` lists — spec §32 notes that not every noun is a natural existential subject (abstract nouns usually aren't) so a future pass could filter the main noun lists and expose them here instead of hand-picking 6 entries; (b) the `fo'i` classifier preposed on the noun (`ha fo'i niu` = a single coconut, `ha fo'i moli` = an orange) — needs a `takes_fo_i_classifier` flag on noun entries and a preposed-classifier slot on existential_noun.next; (c) `existential_noun` + relative clause (spec §32 sub-pattern `ʻOku ʻi ai ha tangata ʻokú ne ngāue heni` = there is a man who works here) — needs §35 relative_clause_extension.

next.label: Add location (ʻi + place)
next.context_hint: Say where it is — ʻi + heni / fale / kolo / tahi (noun-class auto-rendering applies)

next.label: Add time word (when)
next.context_hint: Say when — ʻapongipongi (tomorrow), ʻaho ni (today), etc.

next.label: Add adjective (big, new, bad...)
next.context_hint: Describe the thing — ʻOku ʻi ai ha vaka lahi (there is a big boat)

next.label: Add purpose (in order to...)
next.context_hint: Say WHY — ʻOku ʻi ai ha taha ke fai ha ngāue (there is someone to do the work)

next.label: Add emphatic purpose (koeʻuhi ke... so that...)
next.context_hint: Emphasize WHY with koeʻuhi ke — the stronger form of ke

have_head.label: Have construction head (ʻoku ʻi ai / ʻoku ʻikai + possessive)
have_head.description: Fixed-phrase head for the `have_construction` entry point (spec §36 Ch 29). Tongan has no verb 'to have'; instead the existential `ʻi ai` combines with a possessive pronoun. 8 compound entries: 4 affirmative tense variants (ʻOku ʻi ai, Naʻe ʻi ai, Kuo ʻi ai, ʻE ʻi ai) and 4 negated variants (ʻOku ʻikai, Naʻe ʻikai, Kuo ʻikai, ʻE ʻikai). Negated entries drop `ʻi ai` entirely (spec §36 examples confirm this). The render pass reads two signals: (1) the `negated` tag on the head entry → always indefinite forms; (2) the walker's terminator — FINISH_QUESTION on an affirmative head also triggers indefinite forms (questions about having something use indefinite possessives). Affirmative + FINISH_STATEMENT uses the standard 2A.6 definite possessive_forms substitution. Required_next to possessive_pronoun (reuses the shared §22 node). Deliberate deferrals: (a) numeral extensions after the possessed noun (`ʻeku hele ʻe ua` = I have two knives — needs cardinal_numeral extension edge on possessive_head_noun); (b) location continuation (`ʻi heni` after the have phrase); (c) past/perfect/future negative tense validation against attested examples (only present negative explicitly attested in §36); (d) `pē taha` (just one) modifier after the noun.

attributive_adjective.label: Attributive adjective (postposed)
attributive_adjective.description: Postposed attributive adjective slot per spec §33 (Ch 35). Spec §33 formalizes the slot that earlier sections (§21 Equational, §29 Plural, §31 Comparison) have been using informally: an attributive adjective describes a noun directly (rather than acting as a stative verb in its own clause), and the Tongan position is AFTER the noun — `(article) + noun + adjective`, opposite of English word order. 7 common postposed adjectives: `lahi` (big), `siʻi` (small), `foʻou` (new), `kovi` (bad), `lelei` (good), `masiva` (poor), `mālohi` (strong). Each entry tagged `adjective` + `postposed` for future tag-based reuse — tag-based reuse is preferred over a new `adjective_position` boolean field per the §33 schema-additions note, since the current slice only needs to identify postposed adjectives and the preposed closed-list is deferred. Attached as an extension on two noun-bearing nodes: `prep_phrase.next` (the central noun-bearing slot — walks through preposition → prep_phrase → adjective, covering `ʻOku ou ʻi ha fale foʻou` = I am in a new house) and `existential_noun.next` (2C.4b's existential chain — covers `ʻOku ʻi ai ha vaka lahi` = there is a big boat). Walker continuation is semi-terminal: FINISH × 2 + `time_word`, so the time-word slot-order chain continues to work. Deliberate deferrals (documented for future slices): (a) preposed closed-list adjectives `fuʻu` / `kiʻi` / `ʻuluaki` / `muʻaki` / `toe` — spec §33 line 4287 — different slot (before the noun), needs a new preposed-adjective slot on every noun-bearing node; (b) double adjectives (preposed + postposed for emphasis, `ha fuʻu meʻa lahi` = a great big thing, spec §33 line 4339) — blocked on (a); (c) `fuʻu` three-way homonymy: preposed adjective `big`, size intensifier `really` (`fuʻu lahi` = really big, `fuʻu helaʻia` = really tired), and tree classifier `the whole plant of` (`fuʻu mango` = mango tree vs `foʻi mango` = mango fruit) — spec §33 line 4358 — needs a disambiguation mechanism at pick time; (d) color words as both noun and postposed adjective (`hinehina`, `ʻuliʻuli`, `kulokula`, `lanumata`, `engeenga`) — spec §33 line 4385 — defers to a separate slice that handles the definitive-accent interaction on the final vowel in definite noun phrases (`e sote hinehiná` = the white shirt); (e) compound adjectives — three patterns: noun+adj (`sino-mālohi`, `anga-lelei`), verb+noun (`tangata tau`, `tangata ngāue`), and verb+adverb written as one word (`faingofua` / `faingataʻa` / `tuʻumālie` / `tuʻutāmaki`) — spec §33 line 4420 — needs a `compound_adjective: true` flag plus a `parts: []` field for morphological analysis; (f) `loto` compounds for emotional states (`loto lelei`, `loto mamahi`, `loto toʻa`, `loto lahi`, `loto ʻofa`, `loto kovi`, `loto foʻi`) — spec §33 line 4479 — needs a `loto_compound: true` flag; they appear BOTH postposed (`ha tangata loto lahi` = a brave man) AND in the verb slot as a stative (`ʻOku ou loto mamahi` = I am sad), so the entry lives in two places; (g) adjective ordering when multiple postposed adjectives stack — `adjective_order_priority` integer field per spec §33 line 4506; (h) attachment to other noun-bearing nodes beyond `prep_phrase` and `existential_noun` (`object`, `equational_noun`, `possessive_head_noun`, `object_phrase_cleft`, `companion`, etc. — each will be added slice-by-slice); (i) `takes_fuʻu_classifier` boolean flag for plant nouns (parallel to the 2C.4b `takes_foʻi_classifier` awareness item); (j) irregular plural adjective forms (`lalahi` for `lahi` plural, `iiki` for `siʻi` plural — spec §29 documents these; 2C.3d's plural_form tagging pass already tagged `lahi` → `lalahi` on modifier_eq but this slice's attributive_adjective entries do NOT carry plural_form yet — added when the plural_marker node wires up in a future slice).

next.label: Add time word (when)
next.context_hint: Say when — ʻapongipongi (tomorrow), ʻaho ni (today), etc.

tuku_ke_phrase.label: Permission head (tuku ke)
tuku_ke_phrase.description: Fixed-phrase head for the `permission_tuku_ke` entry point (spec §34 Entry point 1, Ch 38). Single compound word `Tuku ke` = let / allow. `tuku` on its own ordinarily means put down / leave / stop; in combination with the `ke` subordinator it takes on the permissive sense (let / allow). This head is tense-neutral — unlike the 2C.3c obligation heads which bake in tense (`ʻOku totonu ke` / `Naʻe totonu ke` / `Kuo pau ke`), spec §34 permission examples do not show a TM before `tuku`, so the compound entry stores just `Tuku ke`. Non-required edges to obligation_pronoun (pronoun path — `Tuku ke u ako`, `Tuku ke ke nofo`) and directly to verb (pronoun-less path — `Tuku ke ʻalu`, `Tuku ke mohe`). The pronoun slot is OPTIONAL, which differs from obligation_should / obligation_must where the pronoun is structurally required; the optionality lets the pronoun-less path model `Tuku ke ʻalu ʻa e fefiné` (let the woman go) where the subject surfaces after the verb as a name phrase. Same compound-form trick 2C.3a / 2C.3b / 2C.3c / 2C.4b use. Deliberate deferrals: (a) composition with §6 `prohibition` for `ʻOua te mou tuku ke lele noaʻia ʻa e fanga puaká` = do not let the pigs run around loose — needs a composition mechanism between entry points (the `prohibition` entry point opens with `ʻOua te` + pronoun + verb, then `tuku_ke_phrase` would slot in where the verb is, but the walker currently has no entry-point-inside-entry-point composition); (b) post-verb name-subject slot (`Tuku ke ʻalu ʻa e fefiné`) — same `verb-first noun-subject` deferral that obligation_should / obligation_must carry, and blocked on the same underlying §14/§16 refactor; (c) the `tuku ke` vs `tuku + object` disambiguation — `tuku` without `ke` can also take a direct object meaning `put down X` (which lives in the normal `verb` slot); this slice keeps `tuku` out of the main `verb.words` list so there is no ambiguity at pick time, and the fact that `tuku_ke_phrase` is an entry-point-level head means users choose the construction upfront rather than disambiguating mid-walk.

next.label: Pick who is allowed (optional)
next.context_hint: Say who gets to do it — Tuku ke u / ke / ne / nau...

next.context_hint: Name the action directly without a pronoun — Tuku ke ʻalu (let [him/her] go)

ofa_ke_phrase.label: Optative head (ʻofa ke / ʻofa pē ke)
ofa_ke_phrase.description: Fixed-phrase head for the `optative_ofa_ke` entry point (spec §34 Entry point 2, Ch 38). Two compound entries: `ʻOfa ke` (may / I hope that — plain form) and `ʻOfa pē ke` (may / I hope that — warmer, more earnest variant with the §3 intensifier `pē` between `ʻofa` and `ke`). `ʻofa` on its own means `to love`; in combination with `ke` it takes on the optative sense of a wish or hope. Same optional-pronoun shape as tuku_ke_phrase: non-required edges to obligation_pronoun (pronoun path — `ʻOfa pē ke mou fiefia` = may you all be happy, which is why 2C.4d adds `mou` to obligation_pronoun) and directly to verb (pronoun-less path — `ʻOfa ke moʻui lelei` with the subject name phrase following the verb). Spec §34 examples: `ʻOfa ke moʻui lelei ʻa e fonuá ni` (may this country prosper), `ʻOfa pē ke tau aʻusia e taʻu foʻoú` (may we all live until the new year — note `tau` in the pronoun slot), `ʻOfa pē ke mou fiefia ʻi homau kiʻi motú` (may you all be happy on our little island). Deliberate deferrals: (a) the `ʻofa pē + tense_marker` sub-pattern spec §34 also documents — `ʻOfa pē ʻoku ʻikai puke` (I hope he is not sick), `ʻOfa pē kuo nau aʻu ki ai` (I hope they have arrived), `ʻOfa pē té ne foki leleí mai` (I hope he will come back safe) — in this pattern `pē` is followed by a tense marker (`ʻoku` / `kuo` / `te` / `ʻe`) instead of `ke`, expressing hope about a potentially-already-true state rather than a subjunctive wish; it is a structurally distinct shape (the head points at `tense_marker` not at the `ke` paradigm) and needs its own entry point `ofa_pe_indicative` in a later slice; (b) the formal hortative variant `tauange ke` (`Tauange ké ke moʻui ʻo ʻaonga ki he fonuá` = may you live to be of service) — awareness item spec §34, same shape but awaits a `register: formal` schema field to distinguish; (c) post-verb name-subject slot — same deferral as permission_tuku_ke.

next.context_hint: Name the hoped-for state directly — ʻOfa ke moʻui lelei (may [him/her] prosper)

relative_clause_tense.label: Tense Marker (relative clause)
relative_clause_tense.description: Tense marker opening a relative clause sub-walk. The relative clause describes the head noun from the parent ko phrase: Ko e falé eni [ʻoku ne ngāue ai] = This is the house [he works in]; Ko e tamasi'í ia [naʻá ku lea ki ai] = That is the boy [I spoke to]. Spec §35 (Ch 39). The sub-walk reuses the shared pronoun node and the standard verb node; the render pass detects any preposition inside the sub-walk and selects the matching back-reference form: i-family → bare ai (preposition suppressed), ki-family → ki ai, mei-family → mei ai; no preposition → bare ai. Deferred: (a) relative clause phrasal accent on the tense marker (ʻokú, naʻá); (b) verb extensions inside relative clauses (modifiers, prepositions, objects are currently available via the standard verb.next menu but semantically unconstrained); (c) transitive-object relative clause (§19 pattern with definitive accent on verb, no ai); (d) UX: skipping prep_phrase pick inside relative clause (user currently picks a dummy noun that the render pass suppresses).

demonstrative_grid.label: Demonstrative grid (full 4×4)
demonstrative_grid.description: Consolidated demonstrative system per spec §35 (Ch 39). The 4×4 grid organized by function (rows: enclitic, pronoun, prep_object, manner) and person (columns: 1st_near_me, 2nd_near_you, pointing, 3rd_mentioned). 12 cells are populated; 4 cells are structurally empty (enclitics have no pointing/mentioned forms; prep_object and manner have no mentioned forms). This slice declares the node as reference data only — it is deliberately NOT wired as an extension edge in the current graph. Wiring requires the same article/noun split refactor that plural_marker wiring requires (separating article from noun in noun-bearing word lists so the demonstrative can slot in). Once wired, the walker will offer demonstratives as postposed modifiers on noun-bearing nodes. Deferred alongside: (a) relative_clause_extension with ai/ki ai/mei ai back-reference (2C.4e-2 — needs recursive sub-walk); (b) koʻeni + day-of-week temporal specifier (needs day_of_week flag on day-name entries); (c) eni as adverbial 'now' (distinct from the pronoun demonstrative, needs its own adverb entry); (d) enclitic accent-shift render pass (ni/na trigger accent on preceding noun — the data is here but the render logic is deferred); (e) manner demonstrative pehē / verb pehē disambiguation (same spelling, different POS — needs §39 verb pehē to land before the walker can disambiguate); (f) wiring demonstrative_grid as extension edges on noun-bearing nodes.

emphatic_discourse.label: Emphatic discourse particles
emphatic_discourse.description: Two emphatic discourse expressions per spec §35 (Ch 39): naʻa mo (even) and kaeʻumaʻa (and also / not to mention). naʻa mo appears in two surface forms: bare naʻa mo before personal nouns and pronouns, and naʻa mo e (with article) before common nouns — same noun-class auto-rendering as Rule 3. kaeʻumaʻa is a coordinator that joins a noun phrase to the main clause with additive emphasis, distinct from mo (with) and pea (and then). This slice declares the node as reference data only — it is deliberately NOT wired as an extension edge in the current graph. Wiring naʻa mo requires a clause-initial extension slot (it precedes the entire sentence, not a specific node). Wiring kaeʻumaʻa requires a coordinator slot on noun-bearing nodes (it joins an additional noun phrase with emphasis). Deferred alongside: (a) naʻa mo auto-selection between bare and e-article forms (needs the clause-initial wiring first); (b) kaeʻumaʻa + subsequent noun phrase (needs coordinator wiring).

postposed_possessive.label: Postposed possessive pronouns (ʻaʻaku/ʻoʻoku paradigm)
postposed_possessive.description: Complete postposed possessive paradigm per spec §37 (Ch 37). Two parallel paradigm series: ʻe-class (ʻaʻaku, ʻaʻau, ʻaʻana + dual/plural) for things the possessor controls or acts upon, and ho-class (ʻoʻoku, ʻoʻou, ʻoʻona + dual/plural) for things that shelter or characterize the possessor — same doer/receiver principle as preposed possessives (§22/§36). Singular forms use reduplicated preposition (ʻa+ʻa+ku → ʻaʻaku, ʻo+ʻo+ku → ʻoʻoku); dual and plural forms use a single preposition (ʻa+taua → ʻataua). Six singular entries carry a short_form field (ʻaku/ʻau/ʻana, ʻoku/ʻou/ʻona) — the short forms drop the reduplicated syllable and are preferred in the pronominal adjective construction (preposed + postposed for emphasis, shipped in 2C.5e). 2C.5d wires this node into the predicative_possessive entry point (predicative_possessive_head → postposed_possessive → predicative_poss_subject). 2C.5e adds a separate pronominal_adjective node on possessive_head_noun.next; the render pass looks up matching short_form entries from this node's paradigm data. The node is NOT yet wired as an extension edge on noun-bearing nodes — that requires the noun-phrase refactor separating article from noun. Deferred: (a) ʻoku short-form / ʻoku tense-marker disambiguation (separate entries with pos-tag-based disambiguation per spec §37 note); (b) wiring postposed_possessive as extension edges on noun-bearing nodes; (c) short-form substitution in predicative context.

predicative_possessive_head.label: Tense marker (predicative possessive)
predicative_possessive_head.description: Fixed tense marker for predicative possessive per spec §37 (Ch 37). The postposed possessives can function as the verb in a sentence after a tense marker — a stative verb pattern parallel to adjectives. Only ʻOku (present) is attested in the spec §37 predicative table. Deferred: other tense markers (naʻe/kuo/ʻe), negated predicative (ʻoku ʻikai ʻaʻaku eni).

predicative_poss_subject.label: Subject (this / the basket...)
predicative_poss_subject.description: The subject of the predicative possessive sentence per spec §37 (Ch 37). Either a demonstrative (eni = this, ia = that) or ʻa e + definite noun with definitive accent (ʻa e kató = the basket). The ʻa e prefix is the focus marker ʻa + definite article e — per project convention ʻa is never dropped before e. Compound noun-phrase entries store the accent-bearing form directly (no separate render-pass needed). Deferred: (a) full noun inventory (currently a representative sample); (b) ʻa e + proper name subject (ʻa e meʻa ʻa Sione).

whose_question_form.label: Whose? (ʻa hai / ʻo hai)
whose_question_form.description: Whose-question forms per spec §37 (Ch 37). The question word hai (who) combines with the possessive preposition ʻa (e-class) or ʻo (ho-class) to ask whose. Auto-selected by the head noun's possessive_class: the render pass walks backward from this step to find the nearest preceding step with possessive_class and substitutes the matching form. Same backward-walk pattern as possessor_preposition (§22) and benefactive_preposition_ma (§25). For mixed-class nouns the default ʻa hai form is preserved (no mixed key in possessive_forms). Deferred: (a) mixed-class user prompt; (b) full ko e + noun + eni + ʻa hai sentence rendering with definiteness.

exclamatory_ko_ka_head.label: What a tremendous X!
exclamatory_ko_ka_head.description: Pick an exclamation using the ko e X ka ko ha X! pattern (spec §50, Ch 52). Each entry is a complete exclamation — compound entries pre-bake the doubled construction because the graph walker has no template mechanism to render a single-noun pick into the doubled pattern. The first clause uses the semi-definite e (this kind of thing), the second uses the indefinite ha (one of its kind, standing out), producing emphasis through article contrast.

words.tongan: Ko e tangi ka ko ha tangi
Ko e ʻofa ka ko ha ʻofa
Ko e momoko ka ko ha momoko
Ko e māfana ka ko ha māfana
Ko e afā ka ko ha afā

exclamatory_me_a_head.label: X was a [quality] thing!
exclamatory_me_a_head.description: Pick an admiration sentence using the meʻa + modifier + ko e X pattern (spec §50, Ch 52). Each entry is a complete evaluative sentence — compound entries pre-bake the full construction because the graph walker has no template mechanism to render modifier+noun picks into the pattern. The construction uses meʻa (thing) followed by a quality modifier (fakaʻofoʻofa, mālie, lelei) and ko e + definite noun to express admiration. Unlike exclamatory_ko_ka which ends with !, this pattern ends with . (period) — the spec examples consistently use declarative punctuation despite the exclamatory meaning.

words.tongan: Meʻa fakaʻofoʻofa ko e hihilifaki kalauni
words.english: The coronation was a beautiful thing.
The song was a wonderful thing.
The work was a beautiful thing.
The studies were a good thing.
The game was a wonderful thing.
The gathering was a beautiful thing.

adjuncts_hub.label: Adjuncts
adjuncts_hub.description: P1-A4 reference node (not a walkable slot). Its next[] is the verified union of the optional post-verbal adjunct targets; getHubExtensions surfaces these edges on any node flagged route_to_hub, filtered by chapter, edge condition, clause-scoped extensionsTaken, and per-target placement licensing. Behind meta.useAdjunctHub.
